records.ety: [OE. <?/rusten, <?/risten, <?/resten, Icel. <?/r<?/st<?/ to thrust, press, force, compel; perhaps akin to E. threat.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Thrust (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Thrusting.]

defs: To push or drive with force; to drive, force, or impel; to shove; as, to thrust anything with the hand or foot, or with an instrument.
quotes: Into a dungeon thrust, to work with slaves.

defs: To stab; to pierce; -- usually with through.
To make a push; to attack with a pointed weapon; as, a fencer thrusts at his antagonist.
To enter by pushing; to squeeze in.
quotes: And thrust between my father and the god.

defs: To push forward; to come with force; to press on; to intrude.
quotes: As doth an eager hound Thrust to an hind within some covert glade.

usage: Thrust, Push, Shove. Push and shove usually imply the application of force by a body already in contact with the body to be impelled. Thrust, often, but not always, implies the impulse or application of force by a body which is in motion before it reaches the body to be impelled.

defs: A violent push or driving, as with a pointed weapon moved in the direction of its length, or with the hand or foot, or with any instrument; a stab; -- a word much used as a term of fencing.
quotes: [Polites] Pyrrhus with his lance pursues, And often reaches, and his thrusts renews.
One thrust at your pure, pretended mechanism.

defs: The force or pressure of one part of a construction against other parts; especially (Arch.), a horizontal or diagonal outward pressure, as of an arch against its abutments, or of rafters against the wall which support them.
The breaking down of the roof of a gallery under its superincumbent weight.
